message:
test(indexer): prove lexical publish rolls back on Linux sidecar park ENOSPC

Adds a serial, Linux-only regression test that simulates an ENOSPC failure
at a very narrow window on the Linux atomic-publish path: after the
directory swap has moved NEW into the live slot, but before retention has
finished parking OLD at the canonical `.old` sidecar.

What the test asserts:
  - The publish must detect the sidecar-park I/O error, roll the rename
    back, and leave the previously-live generation in place
  - The NEW generation must remain reachable only via the staged tempdir,
    not in the canonical index path (so subsequent readers do not observe
    a half-applied swap)
  - The ENOSPC raw OS error (28) surfaces to the caller so retention
    pressure is attributable rather than silently swallowed

Why this matters:
  The sidecar-park step is the last thing standing between a successful
  swap and a corrupted retention chain. If it fails and we *don't* roll
  back, we can end up with the new generation in the live slot but the
  prior live generation unrecoverable — which breaks the "always recover
  to a known-good index" invariant the publisher promises.

Also adds `#[allow(dead_code)]` on `LexicalPublishRenameSite` since the
enum now has variants that only trigger on the test path; this keeps
clippy clean under `-D warnings` without a feature gate.

Non-Linux: the test is compiled out (`cfg(target_os = "linux")`) because
the atomic sidecar-park semantics are specific to the Linux rename path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
